name: Build a Tax Calculator Library
scenario: You're building utility methods that will be used across multiple financial apps your company is developing — a budgeting app, a salary negotiation tool, and a tax-filing helper. Different teams need the same calculations. Your job: build a TAX UTILITIES class with cleanly designed methods (parameters in, results out, no side effects). This is exactly how real Java libraries are built — Math, String, Collections — all utility methods other code can call. After this project, you'll understand WHY the Java standard library is structured the way it is.
description: Build a TaxUtils class containing several methods for tax-related calculations. Each method should have a clean signature, do ONE thing, and return its result. Then build a demo main() that uses your library to produce a complete tax report.

core.description: `calculateTaxableIncome(double grossIncome, double deductions)` returns income after deductions
`calculateTax(double taxableIncome, double rate)` returns tax amount
`calculateNetIncome(double grossIncome, double tax)` returns income after tax
`getTaxBracket(double income)` returns the bracket NAME as String (using if-else if)
`getEffectiveTaxRate(double income, double tax)` returns the actual percentage rate paid
main() uses ALL methods to produce a tax report for one person
NO class-level static variables — only parameters and returns

bonus.description: Add a `formatCurrency(double amount)` helper method that returns a String like '₹1,23,456.00' (use String.format with %,.2f)
Add `calculateCumulativeTax(double income)` that uses the loop-based cumulative bracket calculation from Module 1, returning the proper total tax
Compare two scenarios in main() — same income, different deductions — showing how deductions reduce tax

data_to_use:
Use these values for your test scenario:

- Name: 'Aisha Khan'
- Gross annual income: ₹1,200,000
- Total deductions: ₹150,000 (HRA, 80C, etc.)
- Single tax rate (for simple version): 15%

For the bonus cumulative version, use 2024 Indian tax slabs:
- 0 - 3L: 0%
- 3L - 6L: 5%
- 6L - 9L: 10%
- 9L - 12L: 15%
- 12L+: 20%

criteria: Each method does ONE thing (10 pts)
All methods use parameters appropriately (10 pts)
Return types match what's being computed (10 pts)
main calls methods in logical order (5 pts)
Results flow correctly between methods (5 pts)

ai_policy_explanation: Limited to 5 AI hints. You've now built simple methods many times. This project is about ASSEMBLY — putting methods together cleanly. The thinking happens in YOUR head: what should each method take? What should it return? If you find yourself wanting to use class-level static variables, STOP and rethink — that's the lazy fix this topic is teaching you to avoid.
submission_instructions: Save as TaxUtils.java. Run it. Try changing the values at the top of main — the methods should still work without code changes (proving they're reusable). Push to GitHub portfolio. This is the kind of utility code you'd see in real Java projects.
tips_for_success: Design before you code: write each method signature on paper FIRST. Inputs in, output out.
Each method should fit in your head — if you can't summarize it in one sentence, it's doing too much.
Don't put println inside calculation methods. Calculations return values; printing is separate.
Test individual methods by changing main() to call just one method and print its return value.
When you find yourself wanting class-level state, ask: 'what parameter could I add to this method instead?'
Bonus formatCurrency hint: `String.format("%,.2f", amount)` gives currency formatting with commas and 2 decimal places.
concepts_reinforced: Method composition (output of one → input of another)
what_youll_learn_by_doing_this: Beyond the syntax, you learn HOW REAL LIBRARIES ARE BUILT. Java's Math class is exactly this pattern — utility methods that take parameters and return results. So is String, so is Integer. After this project, you understand: a 'library' isn't magic. It's just a class with well-designed methods. You could publish your TaxUtils to GitHub and people could import it in their projects. That's how every JAR file you'll ever depend on was built.
extensions_for_advanced_learners: Add error handling for invalid inputs (negative income, etc.)
Add the New vs Old regime comparison for Indian taxes
Add support for multiple tax brackets in a loop (cumulative tax)
Convert to a proper class with private state (preview of OOP)
Write JUnit tests for each method (preview of testing module)
